text: The surgeon releases the endopelvic fascia on the left side, working medially over the prostate. Veins over the prostate are grasped to access the medial aspect of the lateral pelvic fascia or prostatic fascia. Retraction with the fourth robotic arm exposes the prostate posteriorly, with some bleeding observed from the Santorini plexus veins.

The surgeon identifies the lateral pelvic fascia or prostatic fascia, using counter traction with the left hand to better visualize the optimal nerve-sparing plane. The dissection is carried apically and posteriorly around the prostate to preserve nerve integrity.

The surgeon releases the endopelvic fascia on the right side, medial to the veins over the prostate, and laterally. The fourth arm is used to grasp the veins for retraction, while the left hand provides counter traction to expose the prostatic fascia for dissection with scissors.

The surgeon uses a red vessel loop to connect the lateral nerve sparing planes posteriorly, passing it from the right side to the left side along the back of the prostate, demonstrating the complete connection of the nerve sparing plane.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to grasp the bladder superiorly, performing posterior dissection to target the vas deferens. After identifying the vas deferens, the seminal vesicle is located and posterior dissection continues with spot cautery to free the seminal vesicles. Once freed, the seminal vesicles are grasped and retracted superiorly, allowing for the incision of Denonvilliers' fascia.
The surgeon creates a plane just underneath the prostate and carries it apically until encountering the red vessel loop. The dissection continues laterally to form a thin prostatic pedicle.

The surgeon dissects the lateral bladder pedicles, which are blood vessels branching from the internal iliac artery and located just lateral to the ureter. The bladder is retracted superiorly, and a vessel sealer is used to ligate the bladder pedicles laterally.

The surgeon stays below the ureters, using the fourth arm to re-retract and optimize exposure. Upon reaching the prostate pedicle, both ends of the red vessel loop are grasped to thin out the prostate pedicle.

The surgeon retracts the red vessel loop to thin out the prostate pedicle. A weck clip is applied to the pedicle, and scissors are used to sharply transect it. The pedicle is sufficiently thinned to allow transection with one to two weck clips.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to ligate the lateral bladder pedicles on the right side, followed by the posterior bladder pedicles, while retracting with the fourth arm and staying underneath the ureter. Upon reaching the prostate pedicle, the vessel loop is grasped and retracted to thin out the pedicle for the application of a weck clip.

The surgeon applies one to two weck clips to the dissected prostate pedicle. The bladder is dropped, and the urethra is transected, revealing the prostate freed from surrounding tissue and smooth, without nerve tissue. Minor bleeding at the apex bed is controlled with hemostatic agents, preserving nerve integrity.
